title: Dual Extremality 24 PASS: 24 is simultaneously min non-trivial Pisano fixed point and max Carmichael lambda=2 modulus
_fixture_purpose: Certifies the joint extremality of m=24 under the Pisano period operator pi and the Carmichael lambda function. Provides a classical number-theoretic answer to 'why m=24?' for QA. Closes item 5 of the Bateson Learning Levels sketch (Level-III self-improvement fixed point). The joint (pi, lambda) extremality observation is the original contribution; both halves individually are classical.
source_attribution: Wall, D.D., 'Fibonacci series modulo m', American Mathematical Monthly 67 (1960), 525-532. OEIS A001175 (Pisano periods), OEIS A235702 (Pisano period fixed points = 24*5^k). Carmichael, R.D., 'Note on a new number theory function', Bull. AMS 16 (1910), 232-238. Watson, G.N., 'The problem of the square pyramid', Messenger of Mathematics 48 (1918), 1-22 (cannonball identity). Baez, J., 'My Favorite Numbers: 24', Rankin Lecture, University of Glasgow, 2008. Joint extremality observation: Will Dale + Claude, 2026-04-04.
theorem_statement: m=24 is simultaneously (a) the minimum non-trivial fixed point of the Pisano period operator pi: N -> N, where pi(m) = length of Fibonacci sequence mod m, and (b) the maximum modulus m with Carmichael lambda(m) = 2. Furthermore, pi(9) = 24, so the QA theoretical modulus maps to the QA applied modulus in exactly one Pisano step.
original_contribution: The joint (pi, lambda) extremality statement at 24. Both halves individually are classical (OEIS A235702 for Pisano fixed points; Carmichael lambda structure for lambda=2 divisibility). The joint observation — that 24 is simultaneously extremal in BOTH directions, and that the QA choice m=9 is a natural pre-image in the Pisano basin — appears to be original to this project and provides a principled number-theoretic answer to the question 'why m=24?' in Quantum Arithmetic.

key_facts.pisano_min_nontrivial: 24 is the minimum m > 1 with pi(m) = m
key_facts.carmichael_structural_proof: m with lambda(m)=2 must satisfy: all prime factors in {2,3}, v_2(m) <= 3, v_3(m) <= 1. Hence m | 24. Set is {d : d | 24, d >= 3}.
key_facts.qa_bridge: pi(9) = 24; QA theoretical m=9 maps to QA applied m=24 in one Pisano step
key_facts.basin_of_24: pi^-1(24) intersect [1,30] = {6, 9, 12, 16, 18, 24} (six moduli in Pisano-24 basin)
key_facts.24_theorem: For every prime p >= 5, p^2 - 1 is divisible by 24
key_facts.leech_connection: 24 = dimension of Leech lattice = critical dimension of bosonic string - 2 (Baez 2008; Conway-Sloane; monstrous moonshine)

witnesses.note: The star witness. pi(24)=24 AND lambda(24)=2. Minimum non-trivial Pisano FP AND maximum lambda=2 modulus. QA applied modulus.
pi(9) = 24 — QA theoretical modulus maps to applied modulus in one Pisano step. lambda(9)=6 because (Z/9Z)* is cyclic of order 6.
pi(120) = 120. Next element of OEIS A235702 after 24. Confirms sequence 24 * 5^k. lambda(120)=4 so NOT in the lambda=2 set.
12 has lambda=2 (in the lambda=2 set) AND pi(12)=24 (in the Pisano-24 basin), but pi(12) != 12 so 12 is NOT a Pisano FP. 24 = lcm(8,3) = 12*2 strictly dominates 12.
lambda(8) = 2 because (Z/8Z)* = {1,3,5,7} all square to 1. pi(8)=12, NOT a Pisano FP.

witnesses.role: lambda=2 member AND Pisano-24 basin member
witnesses.note: Both lambda=2 and pi=24. Sits in both the Carmichael lambda=2 set AND the Pisano-24 basin — but m=6 is strictly smaller than 24 in both orderings.
m=1: Z/1Z is the zero ring. pi(1)=1 trivially. Excluded from 'non-trivial' Pisano FP counting. lambda(1)=1 (not 2), excluded from lambda=2 set.

basin_of_attraction_24.note: Six distinct moduli in [1, 30] map to 24 under pi. The QA theoretical choice m=9 is one of six natural pre-images. 24 has a rich basin of attraction, not a singleton.

carmichael_lambda_2_structural_proof.proof: If lambda(m) = 2, then (Z/mZ)* has exponent 2, so every unit squares to 1. Write m = 2^a * 3^b * (product of other primes). (i) For any prime p >= 5 dividing m, lambda((Z/pZ)*) = p-1 >= 4 > 2, contradiction. So m = 2^a * 3^b. (ii) lambda(2^a) = 2^(a-2) for a >= 3. For this to equal 2, a <= 3. (iii) lambda(3^b) = 2 * 3^(b-1). For this to be <= 2, b <= 1. So m | 2^3 * 3 = 24. (iv) Checking divisors: lambda(1)=1, lambda(2)=1, lambda(3)=2, lambda(4)=2, lambda(6)=2, lambda(8)=2, lambda(12)=2, lambda(24)=2. Hence {m : lambda(m)=2} = {3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24}.
carmichael_lambda_2_structural_proof.bijection_to_divisors_of_24: Set is exactly the divisors of 24 that are >= 3

connection_to_learning_levels_cert_191.statement: This cert closes item 5 of the Bateson Learning Levels sketch (docs/theory/QA_BATESON_LEARNING_LEVELS_SKETCH.md): 'Self-improvement fixed point check — is m=9 or m=24 a fixed point of a natural Level-III operator?'
connection_to_learning_levels_cert_191.answer: The Pisano period operator pi: N -> N is a natural Level-III self-improvement operator on QA state spaces (it takes a modulus to the length of its Fibonacci orbit). Under pi, m=24 is the minimum non-trivial fixed point, and m=9 is a pre-image reaching the fixed point in exactly one step. QA's modulus choices sit at the two canonical positions in the minimal attractor basin.
connection_to_learning_levels_cert_191.implication_for_cert_191: Level-III stability in the QA kernel self-improvement sense is not an open problem — it has a classical answer. The Pisano fixed point at 24 is the Level-III stable modulus, and the QA choice of m=9 is justified as its canonical pre-image.

convergence_map.fact: 24 is unique n with sum of squares = perfect square
24 = dimension of unique even unimodular lattice with no roots
p^2 - 1 divisible by 24 for primes p >= 5
H^2 - G^2 = 2CF always divisible by 24 for Pythagorean directions
24 is the Level-III self-improvement fixed point closing item 5

cross_references.name: QA Origin of 24
cross_references.relation: Dual derivation of 24 from Pyth-1 and QA-4 geometry; this cert adds a THIRD derivation via Pisano/Carmichael extremality

cross_references.name: QA Septenary Unit Group
cross_references.relation: (Z/9Z)* = Z/6Z is the unit group; pi(9)=24 shows 9 maps to the Pisano FP 24

cross_references.name: QA Bateson Learning Levels
cross_references.relation: Closes item 5 (Level-III self-improvement fixed point). Pisano operator is the natural Level-III operator on moduli.
